cards.front: What does CPU stand for and what is its role?
cards.back: Central Processing Unit. It executes program instructions — the 'brain' of the computer.

cards.front: What is Von Neumann architecture?
cards.back: A computer design where data and instructions are stored together in the same memory. The CPU fetches and executes instructions from this shared memory.

cards.front: What are the three buses in a Von Neumann computer?
cards.back:
1. Data bus — carries data and instructions (bidirectional)
2. Address bus — carries memory addresses (CPU → memory)
3. Control bus — carries timing and control signals (bidirectional)

cards.front: What does the ALU do?
cards.back: The Arithmetic Logic Unit performs arithmetic operations (+, -, ×, ÷) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT).

cards.front: What does the Control Unit (CU) do?
cards.back: It coordinates all CPU activities. It decodes instructions and sends control signals to other components to carry out those instructions.

cards.front: What does the Program Counter (PC) hold?
cards.back: The memory address of the NEXT instruction to be fetched. After each fetch it is incremented to point to the following instruction.

cards.front: What does the MAR hold?
cards.back: Memory Address Register — holds the address of the memory location currently being read from or written to.

cards.front: What does the MDR hold?
cards.back: Memory Data Register — holds the data that has just been fetched from memory, or data waiting to be written to memory.

cards.front: What does the CIR hold?
cards.back: Current Instruction Register — holds the instruction currently being decoded and executed.

cards.front: Describe the three stages of the fetch-decode-execute cycle.
cards.back:
1. Fetch: copy next instruction from memory into the CIR (via MAR/MDR); increment PC.
2. Decode: CU decodes the instruction in the CIR.
3. Execute: carry out the instruction (e.g. ALU performs calculation).

cards.front: Name three factors that affect CPU performance.
cards.back:
1. Clock speed (GHz) — more cycles per second = faster
2. Number of cores — parallel processing
3. Cache size — larger cache = fewer slow RAM accesses

cards.front: What is RAM and what are its key properties?
cards.back: Random Access Memory. Fast, volatile (loses data when power is off), read/write. Holds the currently running programs and data.

cards.front: What is ROM and what are its key properties?
cards.back: Read-Only Memory. Non-volatile (keeps data without power), read-only. Stores firmware like the BIOS/boot instructions.

cards.front: What is virtual memory?
cards.back: A section of the hard drive/SSD used as an extension of RAM when physical RAM is full. Slower than RAM but allows more programs to run simultaneously.

cards.front: Compare HDD and SSD storage.
cards.back:
HDD: spinning magnetic platters, slower, cheaper per GB, moving parts (fragile), large capacity.
SSD: flash memory chips, faster, more expensive per GB, no moving parts (durable), lighter.

cards.front: What are the advantages and disadvantages of cloud storage?
cards.back:
Advantages: accessible anywhere, no physical device, unlimited scalability.
Disadvantages: requires internet, ongoing subscription cost, privacy/security concerns.

cards.front: What is the purpose of the GPU?
cards.back: Graphics Processing Unit — handles rendering of images, video, and 3D graphics. Contains thousands of small cores optimised for parallel tasks.

cards.front: What does the motherboard do?
cards.back: It is the main circuit board of a computer. It physically connects all components (CPU, RAM, storage, expansion cards) and provides communication pathways between them.

cards.front: What is cache memory and why is it used?
cards.back: A small, very fast type of memory built into or near the CPU. It stores frequently accessed data so the CPU doesn't have to wait for slower RAM.

cards.front: What is the difference between primary and secondary storage?
cards.back:
Primary storage (RAM, ROM, cache): directly accessible by CPU, fast, mostly volatile.
Secondary storage (HDD, SSD, optical, cloud): non-volatile, slower, used for long-term data.
